speaker: interviewer
text: --About having fled your hometown at the outbreak of World War II. You arrived in Warsaw. And you found yourself sleeping in a very small synagogue. Can you just carry on with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was [INAUDIBLE] 16, the name of the street where that little synagogue is. And well, like most of us, we're not used to it, to sleep on a bench. And after two weeks-- it took about four weeks till the Germans occupied Warsaw too-- I said, I want to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took my bike and started on my way home. Like, I didn't know what is a war. On the way, it was about Zyrardow, from Warsaw, I don't remember how many kilometers. And me and another man went back to Tomaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there, they stayed some soldiers. And I said to him, look at, there are French soldiers. He said, no, they are Germans. He was an older man. And I said, OK, let's go back. I didn't realize. And right away, one said halt, [GERMAN]. First what they did, took away my bicycle. And then they put us on a field, and we did stay there. Then they start to separate who is Jewish and who is not Jewish. And one Polish boy, he came over to me, to give him the shoes what I did to wear. And I said to him, oh, no, you can't get the shoes from me. And he, I remember, went over to a German and said, he is Jude. In the meantime, I did hide between other people, because there were a lot of people there. And I didn't give him the shoes. Later on, they started to march, that we should march. And I see that there are coming bullets from all over on the field. And I did run, me and some-- two other boys, too. We did run. And we did wind up in a yard, where there was a little veranda. And we did stay there overnight. And then we did proceed to go to Tomaszow by foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: The events that you just described, of the gathering of the Jews in that field, and then there was shooting.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, shooting when we did march. Then we started to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the fate of the others that were left behind on the field?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't-- apparently they-- a lot of people they took later on to Germany from there. I don't know what had happened. But as far as I know, that's what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were the Nazis using machine guns to murder these people on this field? Or the bullets you were hearing, were they coming from one person, from many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a lot of them, a lot of them. They did come from all over. The bullets that did fly, just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people shooting?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I just ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: And those soldiers, the German soldiers that had you assembled there, do you know if those were regular army, or whether they were SS? Us

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. I didn't know in that time what is SS, what is gestapo, and what is plain soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what color their uniforms were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were the green, those green uniforms. This I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately how many people were assembled in that field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, quite a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not only Jews. A lot of-- oh, I would say in my estimate, was for sure about 1,500 or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were many of them killed at that place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the reason you don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I run.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived back to Tomaszow, what then happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I arrived to Tomaszow, I went on [INAUDIBLE] five, where my parents. I didn't go to my brother. And then my brother got to know that I am back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother are we talking about right now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nojech, the oldest brother. But he used to live on [INAUDIBLE] eight. And it happens, where he used to live, he had a very beautiful apartment. And in one of these rooms from the apartment, the Germans took away. And they made in that time-- it's called a [GERMAN] station. They installed something, like telephones or whatever it is. And right away in that time, some journalists did see a sign, a tailoring. And right away they came over to my brother, to make for his wive and-- so he got the ride, and then he did hear that I'm back. So he said to one of the-- I don't know what he was, a captain or whatever it is. And he said, my brother is here. He have to be picked up, because he was afraid to walk. So one German came over with my sister-in-law. But they didn't walk together. She did walk apart, about 10 feet or more. And he did walk. And then they took me back to his place on [INAUDIBLE] eight. But still we didn't walk together. I walked with my sister-in-law and that German. That's so nobody can say something or arrest me or whatever it is. Happens nobody-- I wasn't bothered from nobody. There was no ghetto in that time. Because in that time, they started-- the first day when they went in to Tomaszow, they arrested a lot of young people and sent them to Buchenwald at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that particular action?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't there. That was right when they went into Tomaszow. When I came back from Warsaw one day, it was done already. They told me they took away a cousin of me. And they took away some other people as a matter of fact. I'm going now. And I will see-- I always see them, what they survived from Buchenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your cousin's name that they took away?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nissen Mel, Nissen Mel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mel was his--

speaker: interviewee
text: Second name. He didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that period before the establishment of the ghetto, were there any German persecutions or actions against the Jewish community in Tomaszow, before the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any that came to affect you directly or your family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one thing that did come to my sister-in-law's brother. They looked for him, for no reason at all. Or he did see the Germans, and he did run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on, they did come around, where is he. So he wasn't home. And if somebody runs away after them, they remember, and they did look after him. And one time they came, and they caught him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they used to live in that time already in our apartment-- not in our, in the house. And they took him out. And there were steps to go out on the first floor. And one-- his name was Pichler. He gave him a bullet right here. And he rolled over from the steps and fell down. I didn't see it. But this is their tactic. This is what they did. And I remember his mother did hear the shot. And she run down. And she started to scream. His name was Velvu.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, Velvu, what happened? That German, Pichler, said, what happened, he got crazy. He fell down from the steps, just like this. By whom were you told these events? From who did you get the information about these events?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother! Not my mother, the mother from that--

speaker: interviewer
text: So until the ghetto was established, was it business as usual for the Jews of Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, tell me about what--

speaker: interviewee
text: It couldn't be business. Because they confiscated it right away. And wherever it did some business, just-- so that nobody knows. If one did with the other-- We did work, the tailoring. Because right away we had some from Germany. They did come right away with their wives. And we had some work to do. And we got a special permit that we can walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: From who did you get that special permit?

speaker: interviewee
text: From a German. There was a name, the head of the SS. His name was Grazer. He was six foot four. And his wife, I remember, was small. And we worked for them, and that kept us alive too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all of your brothers and your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, me, my father, and my brother. Everybody did the best what is possible. If you could do favors for somebody, you did. Like my brother-in-law-- the oldest, oldest sister-- he did always outside-- he, how do you call this-- with wool, wool. He always used together and sell it, that he had outside the ghetto. And through that Grazer, my brother did see that he got a green band, beside that Mogen Dovid, a green band, that he could go out from the ghetto and deal and put together and sell it. And not sell it-- the main thing he went out, he always got something to eat. And he could bring in something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I still would like to talk a little bit more about that period before the ghetto was established. Were there were other requirements that the Nazis made on the Jewish community, for instance the wearing of any particular clothing or any particular representation that they were Jews or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had the white with the David star on-- a white and blue star.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was that information communicated to you, that you were required now to wear the star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to wear?

speaker: interviewer
text: How does the information communicated to you, that you were required to wear that star of David?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, they put it on signs all over. And the second, through the Jewish-- Gemeinde, like Congress, not exactly. You know, it was called Jewish Gemeinde. Like there was a president and this. But they wear the same as we did. They just had a little-- they were some that were bad, some were very good too, even the Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that the Jewish council in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish council, yeah. But again, they couldn't be good for everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the signs say about the wearing of the Mogen Dovid? Did it did it state what the result would be, if you did not wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the result, if they would recognize you are not wearing, they could shoot you, as plain as this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How else did the Nazi occupation of your town, before the ghetto, how did it affect the Jewish community? Was there limitations on food? Was there limitations on movement? Was there--

speaker: interviewee
text: You could only walk-- if I remember correctly, I think it was till about 6 o'clock. And it was restricted where to walk. If you did walk and they did see you, there is no-- they could kill you right away, on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- you didn't know. Some time you passed by a soldier or whatever they were, and you always wanted to be nice or whatever. You say good morning or whatever it is. Somebody could say-- could knock out your teeth and say, what, I am your friend? Why are you saying good morning to me? That's what's they-- and some, they answer. And what would you do, when you walked by a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did try not to walk. If I did see a German there on this side, I walked on the other side. Everybody did, not only me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Prior to the establishing of the ghetto, did you have any direct confrontations or were you ever in any danger at the hands of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, one time was-- I was just lucky-- when from the ghetto, they took us to work. We worked for the Germans. And we had to go not on the sidewalk, on the road, with the Jewish kapo. You heard about the kapos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like the Jewish police. And they did this a few times. All of a sudden, you see that he did call-- he was called Pichler, and one was Fox. And one did stay here-- we walked like this-- and one did stay here, and one did stay here. And they walked by. And they want to see what you have it on. You might smuggle in something to the shop. You might smuggle in something to the shop and sell it or whatever it is, in order to get some bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in that time, if they catch somebody with fur, you were killed immediately. And I remember I had on me some fur, in order to take to the shop and sell it there. And somehow, some way, they didn't notice.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they would have noticed whatever it is, I would be killed on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto established in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in 1942 or '41. I think between '41 and '42 was established. Yeah, for sure, in '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were all of your family members-- brothers and sisters and parents-- still in Tomaszow when the ghetto was established?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody had fled the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Except my brother. He went to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my younger brother. His name, Chaskl

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any discussion about Chaskl going to Russia? Or did he just go off without the family knowing that he was leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't hear any discussion. As a matter of fact, a week before, I went there to Russia, to Bialystock. That was occupied by the Russians. And then from Bialystok, I went to Baranovich. And I didn't have where to sleep. To eat, I had. And I said no, that's not for me. I'm going back. And on my way back home, he went at that time to Bialystok. But we didn't meet each other. And I did stay in Tomaszow, and he went to Bialystok. And then he went to Baranovich. And he got arrested. He wanted to go to Vilna, in order to come to the States. Apparently, you could go from Vilna to the states. And then they send him to Russia, I don't know, to Siberia or what. And there he passed away. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How was the community made aware that it had to move itself into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was, that was it. You have to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Germans come around with bullhorns, with microphones? Or were there posters put up? How was the information--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was put up posters. And automatically, the Jewish people they start to be together in one place. That was almost a ghetto. That was-- some people that did live outside, and then they had to move in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family live outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, except my brother's place was outside the ghetto. And then we moved in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older brother, Nojech. We were outside the ghetto until the last day. Until the last--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. You were living with Nojech at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the posters say, directing the Jews to assemble in this area? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall. The only thing I recall, we have to move in. That's all

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went into the ghetto, was there any process of registration?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You were registered by the Jewish-- no, I recall arbeit, arbeit-- working. We were all registered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that registration done by the [INAUDIBLE], the Jewish council?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE], that is right. The Jewish council.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did that registration involve?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they have your-- you're present, whatever. It's never-- they need you, and they have you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the ghetto a closed ghetto in Tomaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were gates and walls constructed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were walls constructed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were actually constructed? Or they were part of the existing buildings?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were constructed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who guarded the gates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Ukrainians, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many gates were there into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean to go in?

speaker: interviewer
text: To go in and to go out.

speaker: interviewee
text: One gate was on [INAUDIBLE], that I know. I don't remember, there were some other gates. From there, you can go in. But you have to go with the Jewish police or some other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying that the guards were Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Latvian and Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gendarmes, military--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Whatever they named them, I don't know what they--

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were in uniform?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a lot in black uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Germans at the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. Maybe there were some Germans, what they were supervising. But mostly I know they were from Litvia, Latvia and Ukraine. That's what I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there Jewish police at the gates as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not all the time. Not all the time, maybe there were some. I don't recall this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you went into the ghetto, did you ever leave the ghetto again? Aside from when the ghetto was liquidated, did you go in and out of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I had to go with a policeman, if I have to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever an occasion when you actually did leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me a typical day in the ghetto for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The day was always-- when you went out, you didn't know if you were coming back.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm talking about right now, your time in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the ghetto. Because you did see a lot of Germans, like in uniform-- SS. They would come in. Like, you went down the street. And whatever said next, next thing you could see, you are dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness that kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not uncommon.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Of an event?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody, I'm sure, did witness this. I witnessed one time, the way they did-- that was unbelievable what they did to one. His name was Shama, I remember. And we did look in through a fence. That was in the ghetto. I don't know, they found something in his house or whatever it is. And they took him down. And they put on a pail, a pail on his head. And they clubbed him to death. And he did beg them, shoot me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, what do you think? A bullet doesn't cost money? Everybody did hear this. That was in that time Pichler. He was the main [INAUDIBLE]. They were Germans. Fox, he was a German Polack. Finally, they gave him a bullet. At the end. You've mentioned Pichler several times. Pichler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was-- was he SS? Was he--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his rank? Do you happen to know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know they called him Meister Pichler.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop there. We'll continue on the next tape.
